title: What Actually Counts as E-Waste?
preview: Not everything with a battery belongs in the trash. Here's the working definition recyclers and regulators use, and why the gray areas matter.
body_md:
## The short definition

**E-waste** (electronic waste) is any discarded device that has a circuit board, a plug, or a battery. That's the practical working definition recyclers use. The technical regulatory definition is narrower — California's SB 20 program specifically covers "Covered Electronic Devices," which means screens (TVs, monitors, laptops, tablets) over a certain size. But for everyday purposes, if it once needed electricity, it's e-waste.

## The scale of the problem

In 2022, the world generated **62 billion kilograms of e-waste**. That's about 7.8 kg per person on the planet — roughly the weight of two laptops every year for every human being alive.

The rate at which we generate it is rising by about 2.6 million metric tons annually. By 2030, the projection is 82 million metric tons per year.

Meanwhile, only **22.3%** of that mass is formally collected and recycled in an environmentally sound way. The other ~78% gets landfilled, incinerated, illegally exported, or stuffed in a junk drawer.

## Common gray areas

A few categories trip people up:

- **Small batteries** (AA, AAA, button cells): technically e-waste, but most jurisdictions handle them through battery-specific drop-off bins, not e-waste channels.
- **Lightbulbs**: CFLs and LEDs contain electronics; old incandescents don't. Different recycling streams.
- **Cables and chargers alone**: yes, they're e-waste. Almost every recycler takes them.
- **Appliances with one chip in them** (a microwave, a coffee maker): borderline. Most California recyclers will take them under "universal waste" rules, but they're not technically SB 20 CEDs.
- **Vehicles with computers in them**: not e-waste — different regulatory regime entirely.

## Why it matters

The $91 billion in raw materials embedded in 2022's e-waste mass — gold, copper, aluminum, rare earths — is mostly being lost. Only about **$19 billion** of that value was recovered through formal recycling. The rest is sitting in landfills or being burned, which is bad both economically and environmentally.

That gap is the actual problem Scraptronic is built around: most people don't recycle electronics not because they don't want to, but because they don't know what counts, where to take it, or that anything inside is worth recovering. Hopefully this site nudges that needle.

title: Why San Diego Has the Infrastructure It Has
preview: California has one of the most aggressive e-waste programs in the country. Here's the law that makes it work and what that means for San Diego specifically.

sources.label: CalRecycle — Covered Electronic Waste Recycling Program
City of San Diego — Household Hazardous Waste

body_md:
## The law that started it all

In 2003, California passed **Senate Bill 20** (the Electronic Waste Recycling Act), which created the country's first state-level e-waste recycling program funded by an advance recycling fee paid at the time of purchase. When you buy a TV or a monitor over a certain size in California, a few dollars of "CRV-like" recycling fee gets baked into the price. That money flows to CalRecycle, which uses it to pay collectors and recyclers a per-pound subsidy for handling the resulting waste.

This is the reason most consumer e-waste recycling in California is **free to drop off**. The recycler is being paid by the state — they don't need to charge you.

## What's been collected

Since the program launched, **about 2.7 billion pounds of e-waste have been collected and managed under SB 20**. There are currently 290 approved collectors and 21 approved recyclers participating.

In 2026, the program expanded to cover **battery-embedded products** — phones, tablets, wireless earbuds, e-cigarettes — primarily to prevent lithium battery fires in waste streams.

## What this means for San Diego

San Diego County participates in SB 20, which is why you see:

- **Urban Corps of San Diego County** operates two free drop-off sites (Moore Street and Escondido) as a CalRecycle-approved collector. They use the state subsidy to fund their job-training mission.
- **San Diego E-Waste** (CEWID 116525) is on the official CalRecycle approved-collector list, which is how they can offer free drop-off six days a week.
- **Dream Electronic Recycling** runs four locations across the county, all free, partly because of SB 20 reimbursement.
- **Goodwill San Diego** participates in the Dell Reconnect partnership, which is technically separate from SB 20 but feeds into the same downstream recyclers.

## The honest gap

There's one notable gap in San Diego's municipal infrastructure: **the City of San Diego's own Household Hazardous Waste Transfer Facility does not accept e-waste**. They handle paint, batteries, fluorescent bulbs, and similar — but not televisions, computers, or phones. They explicitly direct residents to private collectors or to Earth911's search tool.

This is unusual. Most major California cities run their own e-waste drop-off events at minimum. San Diego mostly outsources to the SB 20-funded private collectors, which works fine because there are enough of them — but it does mean residents have to do a little legwork to find one.

That legwork is what this site exists to remove.

title: What Actually Happens After You Drop Off a Laptop
preview: The process from drop-off to recovered metal isn't magic. Here's the rough chain, who does what, and what the byproducts are.

sources.label: EPA — Cleaning Up Electronic Waste (Used Electronics)

body_md:
## Step 1: Collection and triage

When you drop a laptop at a CalRecycle-approved collector, the first thing that happens is sorting. Devices go into rough piles:

- **Working / reusable** (gets refurbished and resold — Goodwill's Dell Reconnect does a lot of this)
- **Display devices** (CRTs and flat panels, which need their own handling because of leaded glass or mercury backlights)
- **General electronics** (most stuff)
- **Batteries** (separated immediately — fire risk)

Devices marked for recycling get a quick data-destruction step. The reputable San Diego recyclers (Simba Recycles, San Diego E-Waste, Terra) will provide a certificate of data destruction on request. The cheap recyclers will physically destroy the drives; the more careful ones use NIST 800-88-compliant wipe procedures first.

## Step 2: Disassembly and shredding

Next, devices are broken down into separable streams. Some of this is manual (cables off, batteries out, screws and screens removed), some is mechanical (industrial shredders that pulverize whatever's left). The shredded output runs through a series of separators:

- **Magnets** pull out steel and other ferrous metals.
- **Eddy-current separators** kick out non-ferrous metals (aluminum, copper, brass) using induced currents.
- **Sieves and air classifiers** sort by particle size and density.
- **Optical and density sorters** separate plastics from each other.

What comes out the other end is a small set of relatively pure material streams: a pile of steel, a pile of aluminum, a pile of copper, a pile of plastic, and a pile of circuit-board fragments that still has all the valuable stuff embedded.

## Step 3: The smelter

The circuit-board fragments are the prize. They get shipped to a precious-metals smelter — companies like **Umicore** in Belgium or **Boliden** in Sweden run the industrial-scale operations that recover gold, silver, palladium, and platinum from board scrap. (There are smaller US operators too, but the heavy lifting is concentrated in a few global refineries.)

At the smelter, the boards go through pyrometallurgical (heat-based) and hydrometallurgical (chemical) processes that progressively concentrate the precious metals. The output is refined ingots of gold, silver, and other metals that go straight back into the manufacturing supply chain — sometimes into the *next* generation of electronics.

## Step 4: The leftovers

Not everything gets recovered. The plastics often go to fuel-substitution programs (burned for energy in cement kilns) or get downcycled into lower-grade products. The flame retardants in older boards are a real environmental concern — modern recyclers handle them carefully, but a lot of e-waste that gets exported to developing countries gets burned in open pits, which is genuinely terrible. This is why "R2-certified" or "e-Stewards-certified" recyclers matter — they're audited to make sure your old laptop isn't ending up in an open burn pit in Ghana.

## The takeaway

The process is real industrial chemistry. Your old laptop *does* get separated into clean material streams, and the gold *does* get recovered. But the upstream choice — picking a certified recycler — is what determines whether it happens responsibly.

title: The Gold in Your Old Laptop (the Actual Numbers)
preview: How much gold is actually in a single circuit board, why nobody's getting rich extracting it themselves, and what the real recovery economics look like.

sources.label: UN Global E-Waste Monitor 2024 — value of recoverable materials
Specialty Metals — recoverable metals from PCB scrap

body_md:
## How much gold is actually in there

A modern desktop motherboard has roughly **0.2 to 0.6 grams of gold** in it. Laptop boards are smaller and contain less. Phones contain even less per device.

Industry estimates for circuit-board scrap as a whole put it at **100 to 300 grams of gold per metric ton** of board material, with high-grade telecom boards reaching 400+ grams per ton. For context, naturally occurring gold ore is mined commercially at concentrations of *5 grams per ton*. Circuit boards are 20–60x richer than gold ore.

At gold prices around $2,000/oz, one metric ton of decent circuit board scrap yields **$7,000–$20,000 of recoverable gold alone**, before counting silver, palladium, copper, and rare earths.

## Why you can't realistically do this yourself

People occasionally ask why they can't just strip the gold off old boards themselves. Three reasons:

1. **The chemistry is genuinely dangerous.** The standard hobbyist process involves aqua regia (a mix of nitric and hydrochloric acid) at high concentrations. Inhaling the fumes will hospitalize you; getting it on your skin will give you serious burns. Even the "safer" alternatives like cyanide leaching are, well, cyanide leaching.
2. **The yield from a few boards is microscopic.** Stripping a single motherboard — assuming you don't poison yourself — gets you maybe $5–10 of gold. After buying the chemicals, that's a net loss.
3. **Refiners pay more for intact boards anyway.** They can recover the gold *plus* the palladium, platinum, and silver, which the home process generally can't. Bringing a stripped board to a buyer gets you less money than handing them the intact board.

This is why the established model is: you bring boards to a recycler (or include them in a mixed e-waste load), the recycler aggregates and ships them to a refiner, and everyone gets paid.

## What this means for the recycling economics

Understanding the precious metal recovery is the answer to a question that comes up a lot: *why do recyclers take electronics for free?*

The answer is that the boards alone — once enough of them are aggregated — pay the recycler more than it costs to process the rest of the device. The other materials (steel, aluminum, plastic) have small positive value or break even. The gold is what makes the math work.

This is also why CRT TVs and big old monitors are the exception. They have very little circuit board relative to their mass, the leaded glass costs money to dispose of, and the precious-metal upside isn't there. That's why CRT recycling actually *costs* money — there's no gold to balance it out.

## The macro number

The **UN Global E-Waste Monitor 2024** estimated that the raw materials in 2022's e-waste were worth **$91 billion**, of which only **$19 billion was actually recovered**. That $72 billion gap is mostly gold, copper, and rare earths that ended up in landfills or got burned for energy instead of being refined. Every recycled motherboard chips away at that gap, even if not by much.

title: How to Prep Your E-Waste Before You Drop It Off
preview: Wipe your data, pull the lithium batteries, don't bother stripping anything else. The short list of things actually worth doing before drop-off.

sources.label: California Department of Toxic Substances Control — Electronic Hazardous Waste

body_md:
## The list, in priority order

### 1. Wipe your data

This is the only step that's genuinely your responsibility. Even certified recyclers occasionally have employees with sticky fingers, and even reputable data-destruction processes have small failure rates. If your laptop or phone has anything sensitive on it (tax returns, saved passwords, family photos), wipe it first.

For most devices the process is straightforward:

- **Phones**: Factory reset. Then sign out of iCloud or Google before you reset to remove the device from your account.
- **Laptops / desktops**: Use the OS's built-in wipe-and-reinstall (`Reset this PC` on Windows; `Erase All Content and Settings` on macOS). On Macs with the T2 chip or Apple Silicon, this is cryptographically thorough.
- **External drives**: Use Disk Utility (Mac) or `cipher /w` (Windows) to overwrite the free space, or just physically destroy the drive — drill through the platter or smash it with a hammer. Cheap, fast, irreversible.

For businesses or anyone paranoid, **NIST SP 800-88** lays out the formal data-sanitization standards. Most San Diego recyclers (Simba, San Diego E-Waste, Terra) will provide a certified-destruction certificate on request if you want a paper trail.

### 2. Pull the lithium batteries out

This one is a safety issue. Lithium-ion battery fires in waste streams are a real and growing problem — they're the leading cause of fires at materials recovery facilities in the US.

For user-removable batteries (most laptops pre-2015, some power tools, e-bikes), pop them out and bag them separately. The recycler can usually take the batteries too, but they want to handle them separately from the rest of the e-waste.

For non-removable batteries (modern phones, MacBooks): leave them be. Don't try to pry them out unless you really know what you're doing — a damaged or punctured lithium cell will catch fire. Just tell the recycler the device contains a battery and let them handle it.

### 3. Bundle accessories with the main device

If you're recycling a laptop, bring the charger and the cables that came with it. Recyclers can sort them efficiently when they're grouped with their parent device, and you save yourself a separate trip for the cable bin.

### 4. Don't bother stripping anything

Resist the urge to disassemble. You're not going to get more money for a partially-disassembled device — most recyclers will just throw it in the bin with everything else. The exception is if you're separating obviously-different materials before going to a *scrap* yard (not an e-waste recycler), in which case you'd be sorting aluminum and copper from a complete teardown. That's a different workflow.

### 5. Plan for CRTs

If you have an old tube TV or a tube monitor sitting in a closet, call ahead. Some San Diego recyclers (Urban Corps, Dream Electronic) take them under the SB 20 program at no charge. Others will charge $30–50 per unit because the leaded glass costs them money to handle. Don't assume, and don't leave them by the curb.

## What you don't need to worry about

- **You don't need to remove screws, labels, asset tags, or warranty stickers.** The recycler doesn't care.
- **You don't need to back up your data before wiping** — that's your job to do before bringing the device, but it's separate from the wipe itself.
- **You don't need an appointment** at most San Diego drop-offs (Urban Corps, Dream, San Diego E-Waste, Simba all accept walk-ins). Check the listing on the [Recycler Map](map.html) for hours.

The whole prep should take 15 minutes per device. That's the entire bar.
